---
title: "STATISTICS 1 FOR DATA SCIENCE AND BUSINESS ANALYTICS"
output: html_document
---

# Module Information

|  |  |
|----|----|
| **Institution:** | National Institute of Business Management |
| **Centre:** | National Innovation Center |
| **Programme:** | Advanced Diploma in Data Science -- Full Time (1st Year) |
| **Module Title:** | STATISTICS 1 FOR DATA SCIENCE AND BUSINESS ANALYTICS |
| **Total Teaching Hours:** | 36 hours |
| **Schedule Note:** | Held on weekdays, except Friday |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Aim of the Module

This module introduces students to the practical foundations of statistics for data science and business analytics. The emphasis is on using statistical thinking to understand data, summarize business and scientific information, model uncertainty, and make evidence-based decisions.

By the end of the module, students should be comfortable moving from raw data to useful insight: visualizing data, describing patterns, understanding probability, working with common probability distributions, and applying one-sample hypothesis testing in realistic data-driven contexts.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Course Content and Teaching Hours

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
| **Topic** | **Practical Focus for Students** | **Teaching Hours** |
|----|----|----|
| [Fundamentals of sets and probability theory](topic1.html) | Using sets, events, Venn diagrams, probability rules, conditional probability, independence, and Bayes' theorem to reason about uncertainty in real problems. | 6 |
| [Introduction to Statistics](topic2.html) | Understanding samples, populations, sampling distributions, the sampling distribution of the sample mean, the central limit theorem, and sampling error. | 6 |
| Introduction to descriptive statistics | Using graphs and numerical summaries to understand data: bar graphs, pie charts, line graphs, ogives, relative frequency tables, dot plots, histograms, stem-and-leaf plots, mean, median, mode, range, interquartile range, outliers, and box-and-whisker plots. | 6 |
| Discrete probability distributions | Identifying and working with discrete random variables and performing probability calculations using discrete distributions. | 6 |
| Continuous probability distributions | Identifying and working with continuous random variables and performing probability calculations using continuous distributions. | 6 |
| Hypothesis testing using one sample for normal and t distributions | Applying null and alternative hypotheses, test statistics, p-values, confidence intervals, and one-sample tests for practical decision-making. | 6 |
| **Total** |  | **36** |
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Intended Learning Outcomes (ILOs)

On completion of this module, a successful student should be able to:

1.  Use bar graphs, pie charts, line graphs and ogives, Venn diagrams, relative frequency tables, joint distributions, dot plots, histograms, and stem-and-leaf plots to visualize data.
2.  Use mean, median, mode, range, interquartile range, outliers, and box-and-whisker plots to analyze data.
3.  Understand the concepts of union, intersection, complement of sets, independent events, and dependent events.
4.  Understand conditional probability, the addition and multiplication rules of probability, and Bayes' theorem.
5.  Understand different discrete and continuous random variables and perform calculations using distributions.
6.  Explain the concepts of sample, population, sampling distribution of the sample mean, central limit theorem, and sampling error.
7.  Understand the concepts of null and alternative hypotheses, test statistics, p-values, and confidence intervals, and perform hypothesis testing involving one sample.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Assessment and Course Work Breakdown

Each module contains the following assessments and course work based on the Outcome Based Learning (OBL) system.

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
| **Assessment Component**                    | **Weight** |
|---------------------------------------------|------------|
| Automated assessment / in-class lab session | 10%        |
| In-class course work 1                      | 25%        |
| In-class course work 2                      | 25%        |
| End module course work                      | 40%        |
| **Total**                                   | **100%**   |
:::

One in-class course work will be individualized, and the other one will be a group evaluation.

To pass the module, students must obtain at least 50% from the final examination, and the overall pass mark is at least 50%.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Grading Criteria

::: {style="text-align: center;"}
| **Range of Marks** | **Grade** | **Grade Point** | **Classification** |
|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| 85--100            | A+        | 4.0             | Distinction        |
| 70--84             | A         | 4.0             | Distinction        |
| 65--69             | A-        | 3.8             | Merit              |
| 60--64             | B+        | 3.3             | Merit              |
| 55--59             | B         | 3.0             | Pass               |
| 50--54             | B-        | 2.7             | Pass               |
| 45--49             | C+        | 2.3             | N/A                |
| 40--44             | C         | 2.0             | N/A                |
| 35--39             | C-        | 1.7             | N/A                |
| 30--34             | D+        | 1.3             | N/A                |
| 25--29             | D         | 1.0             | N/A                |
| 00--24             | E         | 0.0             | N/A                |
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Learning Approach

Students are expected to learn statistics as a practical language for data-based reasoning. Each topic should be studied with examples, calculations, visualizations, and interpretation. In business analytics and data science, the goal is not only to compute an answer, but also to explain what the answer means for a decision.

A successful student should therefore practise:

-   drawing and interpreting statistical graphs;
-   summarizing data using appropriate numerical measures;
-   translating real situations into probability statements;
-   identifying suitable random variables and probability distributions;
-   interpreting uncertainty using sampling distributions, confidence intervals, and p-values;
-   communicating statistical conclusions clearly and responsibly.

# Instructor Information

**Lecturer:** Dr. Rajitha M. Silva\
**Email:** [rajithas\@vl.nibm.lk](mailto:rajithas@vl.nibm.lk)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*This course is effective from 7th May 2026.*
